title: Weekly Cardio Recommendation
description: We recommend around 300 minutes each week of regular cardiovascular-focused exercise.

title: HR Zone 1
description: Aerobic, low Intensity (50-60% of your max heart rate)

title: HR Zone 2
description: Moderate, aerobic Intensity (60-70% of your max heart rate)

title: HR Zone 3
description: Moderate to High Aerobic Intensity (70-80% of max heart rate)

title: HR Zone 4
description: High, Anaerobic (80-90% of max heart rate)

title: HR Zone 5
description: Very High, Anerobic (90-100% of max heart rate)

title: Fuel Sources
description: There are three sources of fuel to move the body...carbs, fats and protein.

title: Anaerobic Movement
description: Movement that lasts from 10 seconds to 2 minutes is generally powered anaerobically (oxygen not required).

title: Aerobic Movement
description: After 2 minutes of exercise, the aerobic system will become the dominant system powering movement.

title: Fuel During Exercise
description: During exercise, we use all three sources of fuel. The dominate source depends on the time and effort of the exercise.

title: Muscle Fuel Storage
description: Some fuel is stored directly in the muscle fibre and is therefore readily available to power movement, quickly.

title: Weekly HIIT
description: For regular heart fitness, a good rule of thumb is to complete one session of true high intense interval training each week. (Zone 4 to 5)

title: Three Energy Systems
description: Phosphagen System for immediate energy. Anaerobic Glycolysis (30 seconds to a few minutes). Aerobic Metabolism using oxygen (longer than a few minutes)

title: Specificity Principle
description: The SPECIFICITY Principle applies to all training...the heart included. Slow exercise trains the aerobic system...intense exercise trains the anaerobic system.

title: Monitor Heart Rate
description: Monitoring your heart rate during exercise is a great way to understand which energy system you are using.

title: Conversation Test
description: How easy it is to carry on a conversation while you exercise helps you to understand how hard your heart is working.

title: Large Muscle Exercise
description: Large muscles of the body require more blood to power exercise...therefore a repeated squat movement can create great demand on your heart.

title: Small Muscle Exercise
description: Exercises using smaller muscles do not tax the heart as much as large muscles...therefore bicep curls will not place as large a demand on your heart as squats.

title: Max Heart Rate
description: The general rule of thumb to calculate your max heart rate is 220 – your age.

title: Runners: Lactate Threshold
description: Runners! Lactate threshold training is tempo training or race pace training. This is done close to your race date.

title: Runners: Interval Training
description: Runners! Interval Training is typically on a 1 to 1 work to rest ratio with each training time 3 to 5 minutes.

title: Runners: Aerobic Base
description: Runners! You should be able to have a conversation while targeting Aerobic Base Training or Long Slow Distance training.

title: HIIT Definition
description: HIIT stands for High Intense Interval Training.

title: HIIT Work-to-Rest
description: A great rest to work ratio for high intense interval training is one to one. i.e. one minute of high intense work followed by one minute of rest or light work.

title: Training Intensity
description: Are you truly training at a high intensity level? Monitor your heart rate to know for sure.

title: MAX VO2
description: MAX VO2 is the maximum amount of oxygen your body can use during intense exercise.

title: Norwegian Protocol
description: The Norwegian Protocol is a scientifically validated exercise work to rest ratio to improve your MAX VO2. Four minutes hard, 3 minutes lite, for four rounds.

title: Improve MAX VO2
description: Improving your MAX VO2 is a scientifically validated way to improve your heart health.
